title: Core Particles III — に, で, へ

meta.focus: Location and movement particles: destination, action location, means, and direction

sections.title: Where, How, and Which Way
sections.summary: Three particles — に, で, and へ — let you say where things exist, where actions happen, how you travel, and which direction you are heading. Each has a distinct job.
sections.whyItMatters: Without these particles you can say what you do, but not where or how you do it. に, で, and へ are the core tools for talking about movement through the world — going to the station, arriving by bus, studying at school.
youWillLearn: に — marking a destination with motion verbs (going TO a place)
に — marking an existence location (something is AT a place)
に — marking a specific time point (ON Monday, AT 3 o'clock)
で — marking the location where an action takes place (studying AT school)
で — marking the means or method used (travelling BY bus)
へ — marking the direction of movement TOWARD a destination

sections.meaning: に — destination / existence location / specific time
sections.explanation: に is the most versatile of the three particles. It has three main uses taught here: (1) with motion verbs it marks the destination — the place you arrive at; (2) with existence verbs (います / あります) it marks the location where something is; (3) with calendar times it marks a fixed, specific point — the same 'pinpoint' idea taught in G3.
notes: Destination (motion): 駅に行きます — going TO the station. に marks the endpoint.
Existence location: ここにいます — I am HERE (at this spot). に marks where the person is.
Time point: 月曜日に来ます — coming ON Monday. に pins the event to a calendar point.
に does NOT mark relative time words (毎日, 今日, 今週). Those float without a particle.
に has further uses not taught here (indirect object: 'giving TO someone'). They share the same 'arrival at a point' logic.

examples.breakdown: に marks the destination — the place the motion arrives at
に marks the location of existence — where the person is

examples.en: I will come next week. (no に — floating time word)
examples.breakdown: 来週 is a relative time word — no に needed

sections.meaning: で — location of action / means or method
sections.explanation: で marks two related ideas: (1) the place where an action actively occurs — not just where something exists, but where you do something; (2) the means, tool, or method by which an action is performed. Both uses answer the question 'how / in what context is this action happening?'
notes: Action location: コンビニで — doing something AT the convenience store (an active event at a place).
Means / method: バスで来ます — coming BY bus. タクシーで行きます — going BY taxi.
Key contrast with に: に marks where something EXISTS or ARRIVES; で marks where an action HAPPENS.
Example: うちにいます (I am at home — existence). Action-at-location uses of で (e.g. doing something at a place) will be reinforced in later lessons when more action verbs are available.
で is introduced in N5.5 — it only appears in content from this point onward.

examples.breakdown: で marks the means of transport — the method of coming
で marks the means — travelling by taxi

examples.en: I came to the station by bus.
examples.breakdown: で marks the means (by bus); に marks the destination. Both particles in one sentence.

sections.meaning: へ — direction of movement (toward a destination)
sections.explanation: へ (pronounced 'e' as a particle) marks the direction of movement — the way you are heading. It emphasises that you are oriented toward the destination rather than focusing on your arrival at it. Like に for motion verbs, へ is used with verbs of movement (行く, 来る). However, へ highlights the journey's direction, while に highlights the endpoint.
notes: へ is used only with motion verbs. It cannot mark existence locations or time.
駅へ行きます = going in the direction of the station (emphasis on heading that way).
駅に行きます = going to the station (emphasis on arriving there).
In everyday conversation both are often interchangeable for simple destinations. See the comparison section for the key distinction.
へ cannot replace に for time expressions or existence: 月曜日へ is NOT correct.

examples.en: I am heading to (toward) the station.
examples.breakdown: へ marks direction — oriented toward the station
へ with 来る — heading in the direction of home

sections.title: に vs へ for Motion Verbs — Arrival vs Direction

items.label: に — arrival at a point
points: Emphasises the ENDPOINT — where you arrive
Used with motion verbs: 行く, 来る
Also used for existence (います/あります) and time — へ cannot do this
に has many uses; motion destination is just one of them

example.en: I go to the station. (arriving there)

items.label: へ — direction / orientation
points: Emphasises the DIRECTION — the way you are heading
Used ONLY with motion verbs — cannot mark existence or time
Implies movement in progress, not necessarily arrival
へ and に overlap for simple destination sentences, but に is broader

example.en: I head toward the station. (moving in that direction)

sections.tip: Both に and へ are often used interchangeably for simple 'going to a place' sentences. The key difference: に can mark existence locations, time points, and indirect objects — へ cannot. When in doubt, に is the safer, more versatile choice.

sections.title: に, で, and へ Together in Natural Sentences

examples.en: I go to the station every day.
examples.note: に marks the destination. 毎日 is a floating time word — no particle needed.

examples.en: My friend is at home right now.
examples.note: に marks the existence location — where the friend is.

examples.en: I go to the station by bus.
examples.note: で marks the method (by bus); に marks the destination. Both appear in one sentence.

examples.en: Last week I headed to the convenience store.
examples.note: へ shows direction of the journey; ました shows the past tense.

sections.title: かいわ 1: 今日 どこに 行きましたか（Where Did You Go Today?）
sections.context: Yamakawa asks Rikizo about his day. They talk about where he went and how he got there.

lines.jp: りきぞさん、今日 どこに 行きましたか。
lines.en: Rikizo-san, where did you go today?

lines.jp: コンビニに 行きました。バスで 行きましたよ。
lines.en: I went to the convenience store. I went by bus.

lines.jp: そうですか。友だちも コンビニに いましたか。
lines.en: Is that so. Was your friend also at the convenience store?

lines.jp: いいえ、友だちは スーパーに いました。
lines.en: No, my friend was at the supermarket.

lines.jp: スーパーは どこに ありますか。
駅の そこに あります。
lines.en: It's near the station, over there.

sections.title: かいわ 2: 来週 どこへ 行きますか（Where Are You Heading Next Week?）
sections.context: The teacher asks students about their plans for next week and how they intend to travel.

lines.jp: 来週 どこへ 行きますか。
lines.en: Where are you heading next week?

lines.jp: 来週 駅へ 行きます。先生も 駅に 来ますか。
lines.en: Next week I am heading to the station. Will you also come to the station, sensei?

lines.jp: はい、来ます。バスで 来ます。
lines.en: Yes, I will come. I will come by bus.

lines.jp: わたしは タクシーで 行きます。バスでは 来ません。
lines.en: I will go by taxi. I won't come by bus.

lines.jp: そうですか。友だちも 来ますか。
lines.en: Is that so. Will your friends also come?

lines.jp: はい、友だちが 来ます。友だちは うちから 来ます。
lines.en: Yes, my friend will come. My friend will come from home.

sections.title: Switch the Particle
sections.instructions: Each sentence uses one particle. Choose the version with the particle changed as directed.

choices: 駅へ 行きます。
駅で 行きます。
駅が 行きます。
駅から 行きます。

items.targetLabel: Change the means to タクシー
items.answer: タクシーで 来ました。
choices: タクシーに 来ました。
タクシーへ 来ました。
タクシーで 来ました。
タクシーから 来ました。

items.targetLabel: Change location to コンビニ
items.answer: コンビニに います。
choices: コンビニで います。
コンビニへ います。
コンビニに います。
コンビニが います。

items.given: スーパーへ 行きます。
items.answer: スーパーに 行きます。
choices: スーパーで 行きます。
スーパーに 行きます。
スーパーから 行きます。
スーパーを 行きます。

items.given: 友だちが 駅に います。
items.targetLabel: Change location: friend is now at home
items.answer: 友だちが うちに います。
choices: 友だちが うちに います。
友だちが うちで います。
友だちが うちへ います。
友だちが うちから います。

items.targetLabel: Specify: coming next week TO the station
items.answer: 来週 駅に 来ます。
choices: 来週 駅で 来ます。
来週 駅が 来ます。
来週 駅に 来ます。
来週 駅から 来ます。

sections.title: Particle Challenge
sections.instructions: Choose the correct particle for each sentence.

items.q: 毎日 駅 ______ 行きます。(I go TO the station every day.)
choices: に
で
へ
が
items.explanation: に marks the destination of a motion verb — the endpoint where you arrive. 'Going to the station' is a destination → に ✓.

items.q: バス ______ 来ました。(I came BY bus.)
choices: で
に
へ
から
items.explanation: で marks the means or method — the tool or vehicle used for an action. 'Came by bus' = method → で ✓.

items.q: 友だちは スーパー ______ います。(My friend is AT the supermarket.)
choices: に
で
へ
も
items.explanation: に marks existence location — where someone IS (います/あります). The friend is existing at the supermarket → に ✓. で would imply an action happening there, not mere presence.

choices: 月曜日に 来ます。
バスで 来ます。
月曜日へ 来ます。
駅に います。
items.explanation: へ cannot mark time expressions — it is only for direction of movement toward a physical place. 月曜日へ is incorrect. The correct form for a specific day is 月曜日に (pin a calendar point).

items.q: コンビニ ______ 行きます。(I am heading TOWARD the convenience store.)
choices: へ
に
で
から
items.explanation: Both へ and に work here for a simple destination, but this question asks for the particle that specifically emphasises direction/orientation. That is へ.

items.q: What is the key difference between に and へ for motion verbs?
choices: に emphasises arrival at the endpoint; へ emphasises direction toward the destination
に is only for buses; へ is only for taxis
に is past tense; へ is present tense
They have completely different meanings and cannot be used with the same verbs
items.answer: に emphasises arrival at the endpoint; へ emphasises direction toward the destination
items.explanation: For motion verbs, に focuses on the endpoint (arriving there); へ focuses on the direction (heading that way). They often overlap for simple destination sentences, but に is broader — it also works for time and existence, which へ cannot.
